title: Perinatal Infections and Chorioamnionitis
authors: Polin RA, Hooven TA, Randis TM / Fanaroff & Martin 2025

blocks.text:
ทำไม neonatologist ต้องเข้าใจ Chorioamnionitis?
Chorioamnionitis เป็นหนึ่งใน most actionable prenatal risk factors for neonatal outcome. (1) 40–70% of preterm births initiated by intra-amniotic infection — ทารกเกือบครึ่งที่เราดูแลใน NICU มาจากมดลูกที่มี infection; (2) EOS risk in preterm ≤35 wk exposed to chorioamnionitis: 5–10x ↑ vs term; (3) Fetal Inflammatory Response Syndrome (FIRS): funisitis + chorionic vasculitis → ↑ risk BPD, PVL, cerebral palsy — ไม่ใช่แค่ infection ระยะสั้น; (4) AAP 2018 revised: 3 approaches to chorioamnionitis-exposed infant — ต้องรู้ทุก approach เพื่อ apply ให้ถูกตาม GA + clinical; (5) Ampicillin + gentamicin standard แต่ NO randomized trial comparing regimens; Ureaplasma/Mycoplasma ซึ่ง = 37% ของ pathogens ไม่ถูก cover โดย standard regimen. เราคือทีมที่รับ downstream consequences ของ infection ในมดลูก — ต้องเข้าใจ mechanism ตั้งแต่ต้น.
เป้าหมายการเรียนรู้วันนี้
แยกประเภท chorioamnionitis: clinical vs subclinical vs histologic — ระบาดวิทยาและความแตกต่างทางคลินิก
ระบุ risk factors ของ clinical chorioamnionitis และ mechanism ที่ทำให้แต่ละปัจจัยเพิ่มความเสี่ยง
อธิบาย microbiology: pathogen frequency (Ureaplasma 30.2%, GBS/Streptococcus 10.5%, E. coli 6.9%) และข้อจำกัดของ standard culture
วิเคราะห์ diagnostic criteria: classic definition, Triple I (2016), ACOG 2022 update — sensitivity/specificity แต่ละ criterion

อธิบาย histopathologic staging: maternal response (subchorionitis → chorionitis → chorioamnionitis → necrotizing) และ fetal response (chorionic vasculitis → umbilical phlebitis → arteritis → funisitis)
อธิบาย pathogenesis: ascending route, innate immune activation (TLR/NLR/NLRP3 inflammasome), cytokine cascade → preterm labor
ระบุ obstetric treatment: antibiotics (ampicillin + gentamicin protocol), CS indication, PPROM antibiotic protocol (ACOG 2020)
วิเคราะห์ AAP 2018 management ของ chorioamnionitis-exposed infant: 3 approaches ≥35 0/7 wk; EOS calculator
เชื่อม chorioamnionitis กับ neonatal outcomes: EOS risk by GA, BPD (multiple hit model), PVL/CP, FIRS
ระบุ clinical traps: epidural fever ≠ chorioamnionitis; Ureaplasma/Mycoplasma not covered by standard antibiotics; anisolated maternal fever = different management
1. Definitions and Epidemiology
1.1 Types of Chorioamnionitis

สามประเภทหลักของ Chorioamnionitis — ต่างกันอย่างไร?
Clinical chorioamnionitis: มีอาการ — complicates 2–10% of all pregnancies; reported incidence varies by GA + diagnostic criteria. Subclinical chorioamnionitis: asymptomatic — preterm labor หรือ completely silent; Ureaplasma/Mycoplasma/Gardnerella predominant; ~25% of infants <1500 g are bacteremic with Ureaplasma/Mycoplasma at birth. Histologic chorioamnionitis: inflammation ที่เห็นใน placental pathology — 3x more common than culture-confirmed clinical chorioamnionitis; prevalence: 50–70% in VLBW, 10–15% at term; 30% of preterm labor intact membranes; 80% of PPROM. Sterile intra-amniotic inflammation: histologic chorioamnionitis WITHOUT positive culture — damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) trigger inflammation without microbes; clinically important; associated with preterm delivery + neonatal morbidity.
1.2 Epidemiology by Gestational Age
Data: Stoll BJ et al. Pediatrics 2010;126:443 — NICHD Neonatal Research Network (extremely preterm infants)
สังเกต: histologic chorioamnionitis พบสูงมากใน extremely preterm (70% at 22 wk) แต่ EOS rate ต่ำกว่ามาก (6%) —
chorioamnionitis ≠ sepsis; EOS เกิดในส่วนน้อยของ exposed infants
ยิ่งอายุครรภ์น้อย: exposure สูง, sepsis risk ต่ำ relative to exposure — แต่ absolute risk สูงกว่า term มาก
1.3 Timing of Inflammatory Histologic Response
Histologic acute chorioamnionitis
cannot occur before amnion-chorion fusion at 11 wk; rare before 20 wk
Before 20 wk: chronic choriodeciduitis can develop — subclinical; linked to preterm birth, BPD, PVL — may be infection or maternal immune response to fetal antigens
Temporal inflammatory cascade (see Section 4): maternal response first (6–12h), then fetal (36–48h+)
2. Risk Factors
2.1 Established Risk Factors

rows: Direct microbial colonization of lower genital tract → ascending pathway; bacteriuria reflects heavier colonization
Nulliparous cervix less efficient → prolonged labor, more digital exams, longer membrane rupture time
IUPC/FSE breach cervical barrier; each device = new portal of entry
Dual relationship: risk factor AND consequence of fetal stress from infection — meconium alters bacterial survival in AF
Each exam introduces bacteria; cumulative risk especially with ruptured membranes
Prolonged labor → more exams, device time, membrane exposure
After ROM, ascending pathway unimpeded; latency period = risk period
Bacterial vaginosis + aerobic vaginitis 1st trimester → adverse outcomes (early PTB, miscarriage, microbial invasion of AC); Elovitz Nat Commun 2019
Women with chorioamnionitis in 1st pregnancy at ↑ risk in subsequent pregnancies — persistent host susceptibility or TLR gene polymorphisms

blocks.text: 2.2 Host Genetic Susceptibility
Gene association studies: SNPs in immunoregulatory genes influence susceptibility — TLR9 rs352140 polymorphism associated with placental inflammation (Karody J Matern Fetal Neonatal Med 2016)
Persistent predisposing condition hypothesis: recurrence across pregnancies suggests underlying host immune variation rather than chance exposure
3. Microbiology
3.1 Polymicrobial Nature
Chorioamnionitis is almost always polymicrobial — single pathogen recovery is atypical
Many pathogens difficult to culture with standard techniques: Ureaplasma, Mycoplasma, anaerobes; broad-range PCR + metagenomic sequencing superior but not routine clinical practice
PCR identifies organisms in amniotic fluid with preterm labor vs PPROM — distribution differs between intact membranes and PPROM groups
3.2 Frequency of Pathogens (Fan et al. Maternal-Fetal Med 2020)

rows: Most common; slow growing; fastidious; NOT covered by standard ampicillin/gent
Acute chorioamnionitis; fulminant EOS at birth
Gram-negative; acute EOS; ampicillin resistance increasing
BV-associated; ascending from lower genital tract
Others (Bacteroides, Prevotella, Candida, Lactobacillus, Enterococcus, Staphylococcus)
Polymicrobial milieu; anaerobes need clindamycin at CS

blocks.text:
Critical Gap: Standard Ampicillin + Gentamicin Does NOT Cover Ureaplasma/Mycoplasma
Ureaplasma spp. + Mycoplasma hominis = ~37% of isolates combined — yet NOT covered by standard ampicillin + gentamicin regimen. Ureaplasma ใน chronic subclinical chorioamnionitis → more intense host inflammatory response than typical pathogens despite lower perceived virulence. Found in amniotic fluid of asymptomatic women at genetic amniocentesis 16–20 wk → adverse outcomes (fetal loss, PTB) despite no symptoms. Azithromycin/clarithromycin = coverage but no RCT has demonstrated clinical benefit from covering these organisms in chorioamnionitis management.
4. Diagnosis
4.1 Clinical Diagnostic Criteria — Evolution
Classic Definition (Gibbs 1977)
Maternal fever ≥38°C (100.4°F) PLUS 2 of:
Maternal leukocytosis
Maternal tachycardia >100/min
Fetal tachycardia >160/min
Uterine tenderness
Foul-smelling or purulent amniotic fluid
Triple I Framework — Higgins et al. 2016 (ACOG/AAP Workshop)
Suspected Triple I: maternal intrapartum fever (any temperature) without clear source PLUS ≥1 of: maternal leukocytosis, purulent cervical drainage, fetal tachycardia
Confirmed Triple I: positive amniotic fluid Gram stain, culture, or histopathology
Rationale: recognizes inflammation ≠ infection always; fever alone = distinct clinical entity
ACOG Current Definition (Committee Opinion 712 / updated)
Suspected intra-amniotic infection: fever ≥39.0°C OR fever 38.0–38.9°C PLUS 1 additional criterion
Isolated maternal fever: ACOG emphasizes — antibiotics of uncertain benefit;
Epidural anesthesia → fever within 1–4h; almost always within 4–6h; noninfectious etiology unless placental inflammation present
Management of isolated fever should be communicated to neonatology team regardless of antibiotic decision
4.2 Sensitivity/Specificity of Individual Clinical Signs
Data: Tita AT, Andrews WW. Clin Perinatol 2010;37:339–354
4.3 Histopathologic Staging — Maternal vs Fetal Response

Histopathology: ลำดับเหตุการณ์ Maternal → Fetal Inflammatory Response
Maternal response (Hours 0–12): starts at chorionic plate undersurface → acute subchorionitis → trophoblast layer of membranous chorion (acute chorionitis) → chorion + amnion (chorioamnionitis 12–36h) → necrotizing chorioamnionitis (stage 3) at 36–48h+. Fetal response (Hours 36–48): fetal vasculitis = diapedesis across vessel wall. Preterm: chorionic plate first (chorionic vasculitis); Term: umbilical vein first (umbilical phlebitis). Stage 1: neutrophils in chorionic vessels + umbilical vein; Stage 2: umbilical artery wall (arteritis); Stage 3: neutrophils in Wharton jelly (arc-like) = funisitis. Key: umbilical ARTERITIS (not phlebitis) → ↑ circulating fetal cytokines = FIRS. Most neutrophils in amniotic fluid + neonatal lung airspaces are FETAL in origin.
FIRS (Fetal Inflammatory Response Syndrome): funisitis + elevated fetal cytokines — independent predictor of: BPD, IVH, PVL, cerebral palsy, ROP
Necrotizing chorioamnionitis (stage 3 maternal): associated with severe neonatal morbidity
Histologic + clinical concordance: 72% of preterm births have concurrent intra-amniotic infection on histology — but clinical signs may be absent (subclinical)
5. Pathogenesis
5.1 Routes of Invasion
Ascending (primary, ~95%): bacteria from lower genital tract through cervix → choriodecidual space → amniotic fluid → amnion → chorion → decidua
Initial site: data suggest initial intra-amniotic entry through restricted cervical region of membranes, then replication, then spread to amnion connective tissue + chorion + decidua
Other routes: retrograde from abdominal cavity via fallopian tubes; hematogenous spread through placenta; iatrogenic (amniocentesis, CVS)
5.2 Cervical Defense Mechanisms
Cervical mucus plug: both anatomic barrier AND antimicrobial peptides (Hein et al. Am J Obstet Gynecol 2002) — bactericidal activity against potential pathogens colonizing vaginal tissues
Fetal membranes + placenta: additional barrier layers; once breached → rapid spread
5.3 Innate Immune Activation → Preterm Labor

Innate Immune Cascade: TLR → Cytokines → Preterm Labor
Pattern recognition: PAMPs of invading bacteria recognized by TLRs (Toll-like receptors) and NLRs (NOD-like receptors) on host epithelial cells, circulating immune cells, and — most important — placental trophoblast cells. TLR pathway: MyD88 signaling → NF-κB → pro-inflammatory cytokines. NLRP3 inflammasome: central mediator of IL-1β elaboration → neutrophil infiltration of pregnancy tissues + initiation of labor (Miller et al. J Reprod Immunol 2022). Cytokine cascade: IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8, TNF-α, MMP → prostaglandin release + cervical ripening + membrane weakening → preterm labor + PPROM. Specific TLR activated (depends on infecting agent) + route of infection determine the inflammatory pathway leading to preterm labor.

Ureaplasma: chronic asymptomatic for weeks; found in amniotic fluid at genetic amniocentesis 16–20 wk → adverse outcomes despite no symptoms; ↑ IL-6, ↑ IL-8, ↑ PTB (Sweeney et al. Clin Microbiol Rev 2017)
Sterile inflammation > microbial infection in: preterm labor + intact membranes, PPROM, asymptomatic short cervix — DAMPs (damage-associated molecular patterns) are proposed major mediators
6. Treatment — Obstetric Management
6.1 Antibiotics
Standard regimen: ampicillin + gentamicin — most commonly prescribed; NO randomized trials comparing antibiotic regimens for intra-amniotic infection (Conde-Agudelo Am J Obstet Gynecol 2020)
Gap: Ureaplasma + Mycoplasma NOT covered; expert panels proposed coverage with azithromycin/clarithromycin but no clinical trial evidence
At cesarean delivery: add clindamycin (or metronidazole) for anaerobic coverage — reduces wound infection, endometritis, pelvic abscess risk
Chorioamnionitis alone = NOT sufficient indication for CS (no evidence improves neonatal outcomes; may increase maternal complications)
Fetal or maternal instability + chorioamnionitis = may indicate CS
6.2 PPROM Antibiotic Protocol (ACOG Practice Bulletin 217, 2020)

PPROM Antibiotic Protocol — ACOG PB 217 (2020)
7-day total course: 48h IV: ampicillin + erythromycin → then oral: amoxicillin + erythromycin to complete 7 days. Goal: reduce progression to chorioamnionitis, prolong pregnancy, ↓ EOS, ↓ need for O2, ↓ brain injury. DO NOT USE co-amoxiclav (ampicillin/clavulanic acid): associated with ↑ NEC risk in neonates (ORACLE trial). Also avoid erythromycin + co-amoxiclav for preterm labor WITH intact membranes: ORACLE II (Kenyon Lancet 2001): unexpected ↑ cerebral palsy at 7-year follow-up. Evidence: Cochrane 2013 (Kenyon, CD001058): antibiotics for PPROM ↓ neonatal infection, O2 need, brain injury, PTB — but NO reduction in infant mortality.
6.3 Antenatal Corticosteroids in Chorioamnionitis
Controversial: theoretical concern — immunosuppressive steroids may exacerbate infection; no RCTs
Observational data: antenatal steroids may reduce neonatal morbidity including severe IVH + PVL even in presence of chorioamnionitis (Been BJOG 2011 meta-analysis; Lee HS PLoS One 2019)
Current practice: most guidelines support giving steroids for lung maturity even with chorioamnionitis at <34 wk — benefit appears to outweigh risk
7. Management of the Chorioamnionitis-Exposed Infant
7.1 AAP 2018 Guidelines — Three Approaches

AAP 2018: 3 Approaches to Chorioamnionitis-Exposed Infant ≥35 0/7 wk
Approach 1 — Categorical Risk Factor Assessment: labs + empirical antibiotics for ALL infants born to women with chorioamnionitis + ANY ill-appearing infant. Limitation: considerable overtreatment of uninfected infants. Approach 2 — Multivariate Risk Assessment (EOS Calculator): established risk factors (chorioamnionitis, GBS status, GA, highest maternal temperature, duration ROM) + infant clinical condition at 6–12h → estimate EOS risk per 1000 live births. Blood culture + enhanced observation: risk ≥1/1000; empiric antibiotics: risk ≥3/1000. Approach 3 — Clinical Signs Only: treat only infants with clinical illness in first 48h — empirical antibiotics or further evaluation. Both Approach 2 + 3 reduce sepsis evaluations but still overtreat substantial numbers. EOS calculator: neonatalsepsiscalculator.kaiserpermanente.org.
7.2 EOS Risk by Gestational Age
≥35 0/7 wk: chorioamnionitis is risk factor for EOS but specificity poor; few develop EOS; ↑ epidural fever → many diagnosed with chorioamnionitis on fever alone
Isolated fever ≥35 wk: consult obstetric provider to determine level of suspicion before routinely treating asymptomatic infants; epidural fever ≠ chorioamnionitis automatically

Term infants + confirmed chorioamnionitis: observation preferred over empiric antibiotics in asymptomatic term newborns — EOS incidence has ↓ dramatically (GBS prophylaxis + intrapartum antibiotics); treating 1 case requires treating hundreds of healthy infants (Jan et al. Pediatrics 2017; Berardi et al. World J Clin Pediatr 2016)
Preterm <34 6/7 wk: empiric treatment still recommended — EOS risk 5–10x ↑ vs term; higher absolute incidence
7.3 Confirmed EOS Rates
Term infants (≥37 wk) with clinical chorioamnionitis: EOS 0.47–1.24% (Benitz Pediatrics 2015; Jackson Pediatr Infect Dis J 2012)
Extremely preterm + chorioamnionitis: EOS 5–10x higher than term rates (Garcia-Munoz Neonatology 2014; Pappas JAMA Pediatr 2014)
Among ≥37 wk with proven EOS (E. coli or GBS): clinical chorioamnionitis documented ~1/3 of medical records; histologic chorioamnionitis ~90% of cases (Stoll NICHD 2010)
8. Outcomes — Neonatal Consequences
8.1 Preterm Birth
Chorioamnionitis initiates 40–70% of preterm births; higher proportion at lower GA

9 lines of evidence (Tita & Andrews 2010): intrauterine infection → PTB in animals; extrauterine infections (pyelonephritis) → PTL; subclinical IAI → PTL; ↑ AF cytokines + MMPs → ↑ PTB risk; antibiotic treatment of IAI in animals prevents prematurity; Rx bacteriuria prevents PTB; ABx for PPROM prolongs gestation; organisms cultured from placentas in PTL; PMN infiltrates in placenta = PTB
Sterile inflammation: more frequently associated with preterm labor + intact membranes, PPROM, short cervix than microbe-associated IAI
8.2 Bronchopulmonary Dysplasia — 'Multiple Hit Model'

Chorioamnionitis + BPD — Multiple Hit Model
Watterberg 1996: chorioamnionitis ↓ RDS but ↑ BPD — pro-inflammatory cytokines accelerate surfactant (IL-6 + LPS → ↑ surfactant production in animal models) but impair alveolar development. Intra-amniotic endotoxin (fetal sheep): inhibited alveolar septation + ↓ alveolar number 20% + ↑ alveolar volume 30% — lung 'primed' but structurally compromised. Meta-analysis (Villamor-Martinez JAMA Netw Open 2019): 244,000 infants: chorioamnionitis ↑ BPD risk (both clinical + histologic); did NOT affect RDS incidence; 64% of chorioamnionitis-BPD risk explained by GA difference + RDS rate. Been et al. J Pediatr 2010: histologic chorioamnionitis + fetal vasculitis → slower O2 weaning + ↓ surfactant response + ↑ BPD. Multiple Hit: antenatal (chorioamnionitis) + postnatal (MV, sepsis, surfactant function) = compound risk for BPD. Van Marter 2002: chorioamnionitis alone ↓ BPD; chorioamnionitis + MV ↑ BPD.
8.3 White Matter Disease / Cerebral Palsy
Controversial: cytokines elevated in cord blood of infants with PVL (multiple studies; meta-analyses confirm association)

ELGAN Study (Pappas JAMA Pediatr 2014; Stoll 2010): ~900 extremely preterm placentas cultured + biopsied: histologic chorioamnionitis → ↑ ventriculomegaly + diparetic cerebral palsy; aerobic/anaerobic organisms from placenta → ↑ OR for both + echolucent lesions on head US; dose-response: ↑ species → ↑ morbidity
Meta-analyses: significant associations between histologic + clinical chorioamnionitis and CP — individual studies inconsistent; fetal inflammatory component (funisitis/vasculitis) appears additive
Principle: chorioamnionitis without fetal inflammation unlikely to cause fetal/neonatal injury; adverse neurodevelopment may reflect prematurity complications rather than infection per se
8.4 Other Neonatal Outcomes

rows: ↑ EOS risk (especially preterm); GBS + E. coli predominant; see Table above
Multiple hit model; surfactant maturation accelerated but structural damage; ↓ response to exogenous surfactant when fetal vasculitis present
FIRS + cytokines → white matter injury; PVL, ventriculomegaly, diparetic CP; ELGAN dose-response
Chronic FIRS → retinal vascular inflammatory injury
Ischemic-inflammatory pathway; ↑ risk in setting of FIRS
Involution + change in cellular composition — transient immunosuppression
Chorioamnionitis + fetal inflammatory component → hemodynamic instability in preterm (cytokine-mediated vasodilation, myocardial depression)

blocks.text: 8.5 Maternal Outcomes
2-fold ↑ abnormal labor progression, ↑ cesarean rate (Venkatesh BJOG 2019)
Postpartum: endometritis, pelvic abscess, wound infection, thromboembolism, bacteremia (Perry Am J Obstet Gynecol MFM 2019)
Postpartum hemorrhage, poor cervical dilation, placental abruption, ICU admission — all more common with chorioamnionitis
9. Algorithm — EOS Risk Assessment in Chorioamnionitis-Exposed Infant

rows: Empiric ampicillin + gentamicin; blood culture; full sepsis evaluation
≥35 0/7 wk + well-appearing + isolated maternal fever (no other criteria)
Consult OB re: level of suspicion; consider Approach 2 or 3; observation may be sufficient
Choose Approach 1, 2, or 3 per institutional protocol; Approach 2/3 reduce unnecessary antibiotic exposure
≥35 0/7 wk + chorioamnionitis + clinical signs of illness
Blood culture + empiric antibiotics regardless of approach used
Any GA + confirmed chorioamnionitis + EOS calculator ≥3/1000 live births
Empiric antibiotics; blood culture; LP if clinically indicated
Any GA + confirmed chorioamnionitis + EOS calculator 1–2.99/1000
Blood culture + enhanced observation (vital signs q4–8h for 48h)

blocks.text: 10. NICU Practical — การดูแลทารกที่ Expose to Chorioamnionitis
10.1 รับทารกจาก Clinical Chorioamnionitis
Know what the OB knows: ถามว่า chorioamnionitis diagnosed from fever + criteria หรือ isolated fever? epidural ใช้ไหม? ผลนี้เปลี่ยน approach ได้มาก
Blood culture ก่อน antibiotics: ทำ peripheral blood culture volume ≥1 mL ก่อน antibiotic เสมอ
Ampicillin + gentamicin standard: ampicillin 50 mg/kg/dose IV q12h (term) หรือ q8–12h (preterm); gentamicin per GA/PNA-based dosing table
Duration antibiotics: หาก culture ไม่ขึ้น + clinically well at 36–48h → discontinue (culture-negative rule out)
Monitor: CBS, CRP at 12–24h: CBC neutrophil ratio + CRP ↑ sensitivity together; negative predictive value for EOS >99% ที่ 12h หาก stable
FIRS signs to watch: hemodynamic instability (hypotension, poor perfusion), respiratory distress, temperature instability, hypoglycemia, metabolic acidosis
10.2 Ureaplasma / Mycoplasma Exposure
Standard regimen ไม่ครอบคลุม: consider adding azithromycin ในทารก VLBW ที่มีปัจจัยเสี่ยงสูง (ยังไม่มี RCT guideline)
Ureaplasma ใน BAL ของทารก preterm ที่ develop BPD: พบสูง — บางศูนย์ตรวจ Ureaplasma culture/PCR จาก ET secretions ใน ELBW
Evidence ยัง limited: ORACLE trials, NICHD network data — ยังไม่ recommend routine prophylaxis
10.3 PPROM Exposure
Remind OB team: avoid co-amoxiclav (↑ NEC risk); ACOG PB 217: ampicillin + erythromycin 48h IV → oral x7 days
ทารกจาก PPROM + chorioamnionitis: double risk — EOS risk elevated; watch for sepsis + lung disease (↑ surfactant dysfunction if fetal vasculitis)
Coordinate: neonatology + OB + MFM ก่อนคลอด → family meeting, delivery room plan, antibiotic stewardship plan
10.4 Placental Pathology
Request placental pathology เสมอ ในทารก preterm หรือ unexplained neonatal illness: funisitis / chorionic vasculitis = FIRS = ↑ long-term neurodevelopmental surveillance
Histologic stage correlates with outcome: fetal vasculitis (stage 2–3) + MV → ↑ BPD (Been 2010)
11. 5 Bedside Pearls

PEARL 1 — Epidural Fever ≠ Chorioamnionitis — ต้องแยกก่อน treat
Epidural anesthesia: fever ภายใน 1–4h, almost always within 4–6h; most noninfectious — placental inflammation อาจเร่ง fever แต่ไม่ใช่ infection เสมอ. ก่อน empiric antibiotic ใน asymptomatic term infant: consult OB re: level of suspicion; isolated fever without other criteria = consult + observation, NOT automatic treatment. การ overtreat ทารกจาก epidural fever → ↑ antibiotic exposure, altered microbiome, ↑ antibiotic resistance — harm ไม่ใช่ benefit ถ้า infection probability ต่ำ.

PEARL 2 — Standard Ampicillin + Gentamicin ไม่ครอบ Ureaplasma/Mycoplasma (37% ของ pathogens)
Ureaplasma 30.2% + Mycoplasma 6.4% = 37% combined — NOT covered. ทารก ELBW ที่มาจาก subclinical chorioamnionitis อาจ bacteremic กับ Ureaplasma โดยไม่มี clinical signs ชัด. ถ้า VLBW + unexplained BPD/pulmonary deterioration ใน 1st week → consider Ureaplasma BAL culture/PCR + consult re: azithromycin. ยังไม่มี RCT guideline แต่เป็น knowledge gap ที่ต้องรู้.

PEARL 3 — FIRS = Funisitis + Cytokines → Long-term Brain + Lung Risk
Chorioamnionitis โดยไม่มี fetal inflammation unlikely to cause neonatal injury. FIRS = umbilical arteritis (not phlebitis) → ↑ fetal cytokines → ↑ BPD, PVL, IVH, ROP, NEC. Request placental pathology in ALL preterm infants — stage ของ fetal vasculitis (stage 1–3) = นัดติดตาม neurodevelopment ยาว. ELGAN study: dose-response — ↑ species recovered → ↑ OR for ventriculomegaly + diparetic CP. แจ้ง parents ให้ชัดเจน: chorioamnionitis = risk factor for long-term outcome, ไม่ใช่แค่ infection ระยะสั้น.

PEARL 4 — EOS Calculator: ลด Unnecessary Antibiotics ใน Term Infant ได้ชัดเจน
EOS calculator (neonatalsepsiscalculator.kaiserpermanente.org) + Approach 2/3 ลด sepsis evaluations + antibiotic courses โดยไม่ increase missed EOS. Input: GA, maternal GBS status, chorioamnionitis diagnosis, highest maternal temperature, ROM duration, infant clinical appearance at 6–12h. Output: EOS risk/1000 births. <1/1000 = routine care; 1–2.99/1000 = blood culture + enhanced observation; ≥3/1000 = empiric antibiotics. ใช้ approach นี้ใน ≥35 wk well-appearing infants — Approach 1 (treat all) leads to unnecessary treatment of ≥95% of infants with true chorioamnionitis exposure.

PEARL 5 — BPD Multiple Hit: Chorioamnionitis + MV Together = Worst Outcome
Van Marter 2002: chorioamnionitis ALONE → ↓ BPD (pro-inflammatory = maturation signal); chorioamnionitis + MV → ↑ BPD. Been 2010: histologic chorioamnionitis + fetal vasculitis → ↓ surfactant response + slower O2 weaning + ↑ BPD. 'Primed' lung: structurally immature lung exposed to inflammation in utero then re-hit by MV postnatally = 3-hit model (Kallapur 2021). Clinical implication: minimize MV duration + O2 in VLBW from chorioamnionitis — NIPPV/CPAP-first strategy ยิ่งสำคัญในกลุ่มนี้.
12. Evidence Update — Key Studies

Tita AT & Andrews WW Clin Perinatol 2010;37:339: comprehensive review; diagnostic criteria sensitivity/specificity table; pathogenesis model (Fig. 24.5) — foundational reference for clinical management
Stoll BJ et al Pediatrics 2010;126:443 (NICHD NRN): rates of histologic/clinical chorioamnionitis + EOS by GA 22–28 wk — Table 24.1 data; EOS 1–6% even at extreme prematurity
Higgins RD et al Obstet Gynecol 2016;127:426: workshop summary proposing Triple I terminology; distinguishes infection from inflammation; isolated fever = distinct entity
Puopolo KM et al Pediatrics 2018;142:e20182896 + e20182894: AAP 2018 guidelines — 3 approaches to chorioamnionitis-exposed infant; separated into ≥35 wk and <35 wk reports

Kenyon S et al Lancet 2001;357:989 (ORACLE I + II): antibiotics for PPROM: ↓ neonatal morbidity; co-amoxiclav → ↑ NEC; erythromycin + co-amoxiclav for PTL intact membranes → ↑ cerebral palsy at 7yr — defines what NOT to use
ACOG PB 217 Obstet Gynecol 2020;135:e80: PPROM management; 7-day antibiotic protocol; no co-amoxiclav

Villamor-Martinez E et al JAMA Netw Open 2019;2:e1914611: systematic review + meta-analysis 244,000 infants: chorioamnionitis ↑ BPD; clinical + histologic both significant; 64% of BPD risk explained by GA + RDS differences
Been JV et al J Pediatr 2010;156:10: histologic chorioamnionitis + fetal vasculitis → ↓ surfactant response + slower O2 weaning + ↑ BPD — supports multiple hit model
Watterberg KL et al Pediatrics 1996;97:210: original chorioamnionitis + lung: ↓ RDS but ↑ BPD — defining paper that launched field
Van Marter LJ et al J Pediatr 2002;140:171: chorioamnionitis + MV + postnatal sepsis as modulators of CLD; MV amplifies chorioamnionitis-BPD risk
Pappas A et al JAMA Pediatr 2014;168:137 (ELGAN): ~900 placentas: histologic chorioamnionitis → ventriculomegaly + diparetic CP; organisms recovered → ↑ OR; dose-response for number of species
Miller AS et al J Reprod Immunol 2022;149:103457: NLRP3 inflammasome: central mediator of IL-1β in fetal membranes → neutrophil infiltration + labor initiation — mechanistic pathway
Sweeney EL et al Clin Microbiol Rev 2017;30:349: Ureaplasma as causative agent of chorioamnionitis: comprehensive review; virulence, host response, antibiotic coverage gap
Fan S-R et al Maternal-fetal Medicine 2020;2:223: microbial frequency data from 14 studies (culture + PCR combined) — Fig. 24.2 distribution pie chart; foundational for pathogen spectrum
Jan AI et al Pediatrics 2017;140(1): chorioamnionitis + asymptomatic infants ≥35 wk without empiric antibiotics: safe approach; EOS incidence very low — supports observation strategy
Wortham JM et al Pediatrics 2016;137:e20162744: chorioamnionitis + culture-confirmed EOS: defines absolute EOS incidence; strong GA gradient
Romero R et al BJOG 2006;113 suppl 3:17: preterm parturition syndrome; sterile inflammation > microbial in PTL + intact membranes
Elovitz MA et al Nat Commun 2019;10:1305: cervicovaginal microbiota + local immune response modulate spontaneous PTB risk — BV/AV connection to IAI pathway
13. Self-Check Q&A
Q: อธิบายความแตกต่างระหว่าง clinical, subclinical, histologic chorioamnionitis พร้อมตัวอย่าง pathogen ที่เกี่ยวข้อง

ตอบ: Clinical: มีอาการ (fever + criteria); GBS, E. coli predominant; fulminant EOS at birth. Subclinical: preterm labor หรือ silent; Ureaplasma/Mycoplasma/Gardnerella predominant; variable neonatal symptoms; ~25% VLBW bacteremic with Ureaplasma. Histologic: pathology-confirmed inflammation; 3x more common than culture-confirmed; 50–70% VLBW; 80% PPROM; may be with or without symptoms.
Q: Standard ampicillin + gentamicin ครอบคลุม pathogens ใน chorioamnionitis ได้กี่เปอร์เซ็นต์ และ coverage gap คืออะไร?

ตอบ: ครอบคลุม: GBS (10.5%), E. coli (6.9%), Fusobacterium บางส่วน = ประมาณ ~30–40% ของ isolates. ไม่ครอบ: Ureaplasma (30.2%) + Mycoplasma hominis (6.4%) = ~37% รวม. ยังไม่มี RCT แสดงว่าการ cover Ureaplasma ลด clinical outcomes. เพิ่ม clindamycin/metronidazole ที่ CS → ครอบ anaerobes (Fusobacterium, Bacteroides, Prevotella).
Q: Triple I framework (Higgins 2016) กับ ACOG current definition ต่างกันอย่างไร?

ตอบ: Triple I: Suspected = intrapartum fever without clear source + ≥1 of: leukocytosis, purulent discharge, fetal tachycardia; Confirmed = positive AF gram stain/culture/histology. ACOG current: suspected intra-amniotic infection = fever ≥39°C OR fever 38–38.9°C + 1 additional criterion. Key principle both share: isolated maternal fever = DISTINCT entity — antibiotics of uncertain benefit; epidural fever ≠ chorioamnionitis.
Q: Histopathologic staging ของ fetal inflammatory response: stages 1–3 คืออะไร และ stage ใดที่สัมพันธ์กับ ↑ fetal cytokines?

ตอบ: Stage 1: neutrophils in chorionic vessels + umbilical vein vasculitis (phlebitis). Stage 2: neutrophils enter umbilical artery wall (arteritis). Stage 3: neutrophils enter Wharton jelly in arc-like distribution = funisitis. ↑ Fetal cytokines → umbilical ARTERITIS (stage 2–3), NOT phlebitis. ↑ BPD ที่ significant เมื่อ chorionic vasculitis + MV present (Been 2010).
Q: PPROM antibiotic protocol ตาม ACOG 2020: ยาอะไร? ระยะเวลา? ยาอะไรที่ห้ามใช้และทำไม?

ตอบ: Protocol: 48h IV ampicillin + erythromycin → oral amoxicillin + erythromycin → total 7 days. ห้ามใช้: co-amoxiclav (ampicillin/clavulanic acid) → ↑ NEC risk (ORACLE). ห้ามใช้: erythromycin + co-amoxiclav สำหรับ preterm labor ที่ membranes intact → ORACLE II: ↑ cerebral palsy at 7yr (Kenyon Lancet 2001).
Q: AAP 2018 Approach 2 (EOS calculator): เมื่อไหร่ให้ blood culture + enhanced observation? เมื่อไหร่ให้ empiric antibiotics?

ตอบ: Blood culture + enhanced observation: estimated EOS risk ≥1/1000 live births. Empiric antibiotics: estimated EOS risk ≥3/1000 live births. Routine care: risk <1/1000. Calculator: neonatalsepsiscalculator.kaiserpermanente.org — inputs: GA, GBS status, chorioamnionitis diagnosis, highest maternal T°, ROM duration, infant clinical appearance at 6–12h.
Q: Chorioamnionitis กับ BPD: อธิบาย multiple hit model

ตอบ: Hit 1 (Antenatal): chorioamnionitis → IL-6/IL-1β/LPS → accelerate surfactant production (↓ RDS) BUT impair alveolar septation + ↓ alveolar number 20% (structural damage). Hit 2 (Resuscitation): O2 + inflation injury. Hit 3 (Postnatal): MV + postnatal infection + immature antioxidant defenses. Van Marter 2002: chorioamnionitis alone ↓ BPD; + MV → ↑ BPD. Been 2010: fetal vasculitis + MV → ↓ surfactant response. Clinical: minimize MV duration in VLBW from chorioamnionitis.
Q: ELGAN study: chorioamnionitis กับ CP — findings คืออะไรและทำไมสำคัญ?

ตอบ: ~900 ELBW placentas biopsied + cultured: histologic chorioamnionitis → ↑ ventriculomegaly + diparetic CP. Recovery of organisms from placenta → ↑ OR for both + echolucent head US lesions. Dose-response: ↑ species recovered → ↑ odds ratios — strongest evidence linking microbial infection to brain injury. Important: CP risk may reflect prematurity complications (RDS, NEC, IVH) rather than direct infection effect; chorioamnionitis without fetal inflammation unlikely to cause injury independently.
Q: ทารก ELBW มาจากมารดา PPROM 3 สัปดาห์ + chorioamnionitis: NICU management checklist

ตอบ: (1) Blood culture before antibiotics; (2) Ampicillin + gentamicin empiric; (3) Request placental pathology (grade fetal vasculitis); (4) Hemodynamic monitoring: BP, perfusion signs (FIRS); (5) Consider CPAP-first/NIPPV → minimize MV (BPD multiple hit); (6) Ureaplasma awareness: discuss with team re: BAL culture/PCR; (7) Family counseling: long-term neurodevelopmental follow-up (BPD, IVH, CP risk); (8) Stop antibiotics 36–48h if cultures negative + clinically well.
Q: Antenatal corticosteroids ใน chorioamnionitis: ให้หรือไม่ให้? evidence อะไร?

ตอบ: ให้ — observational data supports: ↓ severe IVH + PVL even with chorioamnionitis (Been BJOG 2011 meta-analysis; Lee HS PLoS One 2019). ไม่มี RCT addressing this question directly. Theoretical risk: immunosuppressive steroids exacerbate infection — but evidence shows benefit outweighs risk at <34 wk. Current guideline: give antenatal steroids for lung maturity at <34 wk regardless of chorioamnionitis diagnosis.

Day 122 Preview — Fanaroff Ch.25
บทต่อไป: Chapter 25 — Fanaroff & Martin Ch25. เตรียมตัว: ต่อเนื่องจาก pregnancy + infectious complications — สู่ specific fetal + neonatal infectious syndromes; expect topics covering group B streptococcal disease, neonatal sepsis pathophysiology, specific antimicrobial management, and clinical decision-making for empiric therapy. Today's knowledge of chorioamnionitis pathogenesis, EOS calculator, and antibiotic coverage gaps will be the foundation for next chapter's clinical application.
